agent1_raw: Automated AML alert suppression has elevated stakes — false negatives mean suspicious activity is never investigated. This model's retrospective validation is insufficient for deployment on two foundational grounds. First, training labels (investigator dispositions, SAR linkage) reflect historical policy behavior that changed during the study period due to an enforcement action. The model learned the post-enforcement escalation pattern, not a stable suspiciousness signal. Second, SAR linkage as a target misses activity that was never investigated deeply enough to generate a filing. A model trained to predict SAR linkage inherits historical investigation blind spots. Regulatory and model governance requirements for AML suppression also exceed retrospective workload metrics. A parallel-run with independent second-look review on suppression candidates is required.
agent2_raw: Four issues make this retrospective validation insufficient for AML suppression deployment. (1) Investigator disposition labels reflect enforcement-driven policy changes — the model learned changing policy, not stable typologies. (2) SAR linkage as a target is systematically incomplete — suspicious activity that wasn't fully investigated can't link to a SAR. (3) AML suppression models face elevated governance requirements: scenario analysis, tuning justification, independent testing. Retrospective agreement metrics don't satisfy these. (4) Scenario retuning and workflow changes mean the training alert stream differs from the future production stream. The bank can defend a controlled parallel-run pilot, not automated suppression.
agent3_raw: The most important issue here is that the training labels are policy artifacts. SAR filings and investigator close rates reflect what investigators did during a specific enforcement-pressure regime. The model learned to agree with historical investigators, not to identify truly suspicious activity. If the enforcement environment changes or novel typologies emerge, the model's suppression decisions will track the old policy pattern, not the new risk landscape. The SAR-linkage validation target additionally misses activity that was never deeply investigated. AML suppression requires a higher evidence bar than retrospective agreement with historical decisions. A parallel-run with independent blinded review is the minimum required validation.
synthesis_raw: All three assessors reach empirical_test_agreed. All three identify investigator disposition labels as policy-dependent artifacts from the enforcement-action period and SAR linkage as an incomplete proxy for true suspiciousness. Regulatory validation requirements exceeding retrospective metrics are noted by all three. Scenario retuning is identified as a base-process shift by two of three. ETD: condition = parallel-run without suppression, with independent second-look review on sampled would-suppress alerts, against preset compliance tolerances; supports_critique_if = model misses sanctions-relevant alerts or false positive reduction less than 10%; supports_defense_if = 6-month parallel run detects 95%+ of rule-flagged cases, 20%+ FP reduction, no sanctions misses; ambiguous_if = no sanctions misses but FP reduction only 10-20%, making operational benefit marginal relative to regulatory risk.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Policy-dependent labels — investigator dispositions reflect evolving enforcement-action-driven behavior, not stable suspiciousness ground truth
Issue 2: SAR linkage is incomplete proxy — misses suspicious activity never investigated deeply enough to generate a filing
Issue 3: Regulatory validation standard for AML suppression exceeds retrospective workload and agreement metrics
Issue 4: Scenario retuning and post-enforcement workflow changes mean training alert stream differs from future production alert stream

empirical_test.condition: Parallel-run pilot without suppression, with independent second-look review on sampled would-suppress alerts, comparing missed suspicious activity, SAR outcomes, and validation artifacts against preset compliance tolerances
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: During a parallel-run, the model-assisted pipeline misses at least one sanctions-relevant alert that the rule-based system caught, or the false positive reduction is less than 10%, indicating no meaningful improvement over the existing system
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: A 6-month parallel-run with blinded human reviewers shows the model-assisted pipeline detects at least 95% of cases flagged by the existing rule-based system, with false positive rate reduced by at least 20%, and no sanctions-relevant alerts are missed
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The model catches all sanctions alerts but false positive reduction is between 10-20%, making the operational benefit marginal relative to the regulatory risk of the transition
